title: Hypercalcemia and Hypocalcemia
section: Alterations in Renal and Urinary Tract Function

items.title: How PTH and vitamin D metabolites keep serum calcium within an exquisitely narrow range
items.content:
Calcium is essential for skeletal structure, cellular signaling, cardiac and skeletal muscle function, immune function, and neural signaling, so intracellular and extracellular calcium concentrations are tightly regulated.
Serum calcium is maintained within a very narrow range by feedback mechanisms involving parathyroid hormone (PTH) and the vitamin D metabolites 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D [1,25(OH)2D] and 25-hydroxyvitamin D [25(OH)D].
PTH increases osteocyte production of RANKL, a potent stimulus for osteoclast-mediated bone resorption that mobilizes skeletal calcium, and these feedback loops integrate signals between the parathyroid glands, kidney, intestine, and bone.
keyPoints: Extracellular calcium is kept stable on a timescale of minutes to hours, much more tightly than intracellular calcium
PTH and the vitamin D metabolites 1,25(OH)2D and 25(OH)D are the principal regulators of serum calcium
PTH increases osteocyte RANKL production, driving osteoclast-mediated bone resorption to mobilize skeletal calcium
The parathyroid glands, kidney, intestine, and bone are integrated by these feedback loops to control circulating calcium
items.reference: ETIOLOGY (Hypercalcemia): "Serum calcium concentrations are maintained within an exquisitely narrow range through feedback mechanisms that involve parathyroid hormone (PTH) and the vitamin D metabolites 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D [1,25(OH)2D] and 25-hydroxyvitamin D [25(OH)D]. PTH increases osteocyte production of RANKL, a potent stimulus for osteoclast-mediated bone resorption that mobilizes skeletal calcium. These feedback mechanisms integrate signals between the parathyroid glands, kidney, intestine, and bone to control circulating extracellular calcium (Fig. 57-1; Chap. 421)."

items.title: Primary hyperparathyroidism and malignancy are the two most common causes of hypercalcemia
items.content:
The causes of hypercalcemia can be understood as derangements of the normal feedback mechanisms that regulate serum calcium.
Primary hyperparathyroidism (pHPT) and malignancy are the two most common causes of hypercalcemia overall, although many other diseases must be considered in the differential.
pHPT reflects excess PTH production that is not appropriately suppressed by an elevated serum calcium, almost always due to a nonmalignant neoplastic disorder of the parathyroid glands (a parathyroid adenoma or parathyroid hyperplasia) with impaired feedback inhibition by calcium.
keyPoints: pHPT and malignancy are the two most common causes of hypercalcemia
pHPT involves excess PTH that is not appropriately suppressed by high serum calcium
pHPT is almost always due to a nonmalignant parathyroid adenoma or hyperplasia
Other causes must still be considered even when pHPT or malignancy seem likely
items.reference: ETIOLOGY (Hypercalcemia): "The causes of hypercalcemia can be understood based on derangements in the normal feedback mechanisms that regulate serum calcium (Table 57-1). Primary hyperparathyroidism and malignancy are the two most common causes of hypercalcemia, although other diseases must be considered (Table 57-2). Excess PTH production, which is not appropriately suppressed by increased serum calcium concentrations, occurs in primary hyperparathyroidism (pHPT). pHPT is almost always due to a nonmalignant neoplastic disorder of the parathyroid glands (a parathyroid adenoma or parathyroid hyperplasia) that is associated with increased parathyroid cell mass and impaired feedback inhibition by calcium."

items.title: Why CASR chromatin hypermethylation drives excess PTH secretion in sporadic parathyroid adenomas
items.content:
Genetic causes of pHPT can be identified in roughly 30% of cases even in sporadic, nonfamilial disease, most often affecting cyclin D1 or menin expression.
In about half of sporadic parathyroid adenomas, reduced calcium-sensing receptor (CaSR) expression is caused by CASR chromatin hypermethylation (H3K27me3 and H3K9me3).
Reduced CaSR protein expression both increases adenoma growth and raises the basal calcium level at which PTH secretion is suppressed, so the parathyroid cell effectively 'resets' its set point for calcium-mediated feedback.
keyPoints: About 30% of even sporadic pHPT has identifiable genetic causes, often involving cyclin D1 or menin
CASR chromatin hypermethylation (H3K27me3/H3K9me3) reduces CaSR expression in ~50% of sporadic parathyroid adenomas
Reduced CaSR expression increases adenoma growth and raises the basal point for PTH secretion
This 'reset' calcium set point explains why PTH is not appropriately suppressed despite hypercalcemia
items.reference: ETIOLOGY (Hypercalcemia): "When investigated, genetic causes can be identified in approximately 30% of pHPT cases even in nonfamilial sporadic pHPT, primarily affecting cyclin D1 or menin expression. Moreover, reduced calcium-sensing receptor (CaSR) expression due to CASR chromatin inhibitory H3K27me3 and H3K9me3 hypermethylation is observed in about 50% of sporadic parathyroid adenomas. Reduced CASR protein expression increases both adenoma growth and the basal point for PTH secretion."

items.title: Why familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia must be excluded before parathyroid surgery
items.content:
FHH is an autosomal dominant syndrome most often caused by inactivating mutations in the CASR gene (FHH type 1), with rarer families having mutations in other genes that mediate CaSR signal transduction.
All FHH mutations impair extracellular calcium sensing in both the parathyroid gland and the kidney, producing inappropriate PTH secretion together with decreased urinary calcium excretion.
Because FHH can present with mildly elevated PTH and hypercalcemia that mimics pHPT, it must be considered and excluded — particularly when there is a family history of mild asymptomatic hypercalcemia or a prior unsuccessful parathyroid operation — because parathyroid surgery does not correct FHH.
keyPoints: FHH type 1 is most often caused by autosomal dominant inactivating CASR mutations
FHH mutations impair calcium sensing in both the parathyroid gland and kidney
The result is inappropriate PTH secretion plus decreased urinary calcium excretion
Parathyroid surgery is ineffective in FHH, so it must be excluded before surgery is offered
items.reference: DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (Hypercalcemia): "FHH is an autosomal dominant syndrome most commonly involving inactivating mutations in the CASR gene (FHH type 1), with rare families having mutations in other genes encoding proteins necessary for normal CaSR signal transduction. All FHH mutations impair extracellular calcium sensing in both the parathyroid gland and the kidneys, leading to concomitant inappropriate PTH secretion and decreased urinary calcium excretion... Because individuals with FHH may also present with mildly elevated PTH levels and hypercalcemia, FHH should be considered and excluded because parathyroid surgery is ineffective in this condition."

items.title: PTHrP-secreting tumors mimic PTH's actions on bone and kidney to cause hypercalcemia with suppressed PTH
items.content:
In hypercalcemic disorders with normal parathyroid function, high serum calcium activates the CaSR to suppress PTH secretion — a pattern frequently seen with advanced malignancy.
Many solid tumors produce PTH-related peptide (PTHrP), which shares homology with the first 13 amino acids of PTH and binds the PTH receptor.
By mimicking PTH's actions on bone and kidney, PTHrP causes hypercalcemia even though endogenous PTH is appropriately suppressed.
keyPoints: High serum calcium activates the CaSR to suppress PTH in patients with normal parathyroid function
This pattern (suppressed PTH plus hypercalcemia) is common in advanced malignancy
Many solid tumors secrete PTHrP, which shares homology with the first 13 amino acids of PTH
PTHrP binds the PTH receptor and mimics PTH's effects on bone and kidney, producing hypercalcemia
items.reference: ETIOLOGY (Hypercalcemia): "In hypercalcemic disorders with normal parathyroid function, PTH levels are suppressed by high serum calcium levels that activate the CaSR to inhibit parathyroid gland PTH production (Fig. 57-1). This is frequently encountered in patients with advanced malignancy. Many solid tumors produce PTH-related peptide (PTHrP). Once processed and secreted, PTHrP shares homology with the first 13 amino acids of PTH and binds the PTH receptor, thus mimicking effects of PTH on bone and kidney to cause hypercalcemia."

items.title: How macrophage CYP27B1 activity in granulomatous disease and lymphoma causes hypercalcemia with suppressed PTH
items.content:
The hypercalcemia of lymphoma or granulomatous disease (sarcoidosis, tuberculosis, foreign body reaction) is caused by extrarenal conversion of 25(OH)D to the more potent, pro-calcific hormone 1,25(OH)2D by macrophage CYP27B1 activity.
This is a feature of local innate immune regulation by vitamin D that operates independently of the normal parathyroid-kidney-bone feedback loops.
The chronic 1,25(OH)D production enhances intestinal calcium absorption, producing hypercalcemia with suppressed PTH.
keyPoints: Macrophages within granulomas express CYP27B1, converting 25(OH)D to 1,25(OH)2D
This extrarenal 1,25(OH)2D production occurs in sarcoidosis, tuberculosis, foreign body reaction, and lymphoma
It is part of local innate immune regulation, independent of the normal calcium feedback loop
Chronic 1,25(OH)2D production enhances intestinal calcium absorption, causing hypercalcemia with suppressed PTH
items.reference: ETIOLOGY (Hypercalcemia): "The hypercalcemia associated with lymphoma or with granulomatous disease (e.g., sarcoidosis, tuberculosis, foreign body reaction) is caused by extrarenal conversion of 25(OH)D to the more potent pro-calcific hormone 1,25(OH)2D by macrophage CYP27B1 activity. The latter is a feature of local innate immune regulation by vitamin D that operates independent of the normal negative feedback loops outlined in Figure 57-1. In granulomatous disorders, this chronic 1,25(OH)D production enhances intestinal calcium absorption, resulting in hypercalcemia and suppressed PTH."

items.title: Reduced 1,25(OH)D catabolism from CYP24A1 deficiency is a distinct genetic cause of hypercalcemia
items.content:
1,25(OH)2D is normally inactivated by the 24-hydroxylase enzyme CYP24A1.
Genetic deficiency of CYP24A1 reduces 1,25(OH)D catabolism, allowing 1,25(OH)2D levels to remain inappropriately elevated and driving hypercalcemia through enhanced intestinal calcium absorption.
This mechanism is distinct from acute vitamin D intoxication, which depends on acute displacement of 25(OH)D from its plasma binding protein rather than on impaired catabolism of 1,25(OH)2D.
keyPoints: CYP24A1 is the 24-hydroxylase that normally inactivates 1,25(OH)2D
Genetic CYP24A1 deficiency reduces 1,25(OH)D catabolism, causing hypercalcemia
This is mechanistically distinct from acute vitamin D intoxication
Inhibiting 1,25(OH)2D synthesis (e.g., with ketoconazole or fluconazole) can help control hypercalcemia in CYP24A1 deficiency
items.reference: ETIOLOGY (Hypercalcemia): "Reduced 1,25(OH)D catabolism due to genetic deficiency in the inactivating 24-hydroxylase CYP24A1 can also cause hypercalcemia."

items.subtopic: Vitamin D Intoxication — Mechanism and Duration
items.title: How acute displacement of 25(OH)D from vitamin D binding protein causes prolonged hypercalcemia in vitamin D intoxication
items.content:
Excessive vitamin D ingestion rapidly displaces the large pool of 25(OH)D that is normally sequestered by plasma vitamin D binding protein (DBP).
Although 25(OH)D is about 500-fold less potent than 1,25(OH)D at the vitamin D receptor, the acute displacement exposes tissues — especially the small intestine — to enough 25(OH)D to directly activate the receptor, increase transcellular calcium absorption, and drive hypercalcemia.
Because 1,25(OH)D is normally metabolized within a day or two but vitamin D and 25(OH)D have much longer half-lives, hypercalcemia from vitamin D intoxication can persist for several weeks even after the source is stopped.
keyPoints: Excess vitamin D displaces a large pool of 25(OH)D from plasma DBP
25(OH)D is ~500-fold less potent than 1,25(OH)D, but acute displacement still raises free 25(OH)D enough to activate intestinal vitamin D receptors
This increases transcellular calcium absorption and drives hypercalcemia
Because vitamin D and 25(OH)D have long half-lives compared with 1,25(OH)D, hypercalcemia can persist for weeks
items.reference: ETIOLOGY (Hypercalcemia): "Excessive vitamin D ingestion rapidly displaces this large pool of 25(OH)D normally sequestered by plasma DBP. Although the potency of 25(OH)D for the cellular vitamin D receptor is 500-fold less than 1,25(OH)D, acute displacement of 25(OH)D from DBP by excessive exogenous vitamin D exposes tissues to 25(OH)D concentrations sufficient to directly activate the receptor in small intestine, increase transcellular calcium absorption, and drive hypercalcemia. While 1,25(OH)D is normally metabolized within a day or two, the longer half-lives of vitamin D and 25(OH)D can prolong hypercalcemia from vitamin D intoxication for several weeks."

items.title: Bone-resorption disorders, exogenous calcium overload, immobilization, and immune checkpoint inhibitors can also cause hypercalcemia
items.content:
Disorders that directly increase bone calcium mobilization — thyrotoxicosis, Paget's disease of bone, and osteolytic metastases — cause hypercalcemia with suppressed PTH, as does exogenous calcium overload.
Exogenous overload is most often seen with excessive oral calcium intake (milk-alkali syndrome) or with total parenteral nutrition containing excessive calcium; ketogenic diets used for refractory seizures in children also increase bone resorption and can cause hypercalcemia.
Prolonged immobilization causes hypercalcemia by reducing mechanical loading of bone, which increases osteocyte RANKL production and osteoclast-mediated resorption — and in older patients this should prompt a search for an underlying contributor such as pHPT, Paget's disease, or myeloma. Immune checkpoint inhibitors have rarely been associated with hypercalcemia or hypocalcemia depending on whether the resulting autoimmune response activates or inhibits the CaSR.
keyPoints: Thyrotoxicosis, Paget's disease, and osteolytic metastases cause hypercalcemia with suppressed PTH via bone resorption
Exogenous calcium overload (milk-alkali syndrome, excessive TPN calcium, ketogenic diets) is another cause
Prolonged immobilization increases osteocyte RANKL and osteoclast resorption, causing hypercalcemia
In immobilized older patients, look for an underlying contributor such as pHPT, Paget's disease, or myeloma
Immune checkpoint inhibitors can rarely cause hypercalcemia or hypocalcemia via CaSR autoimmunity
items.reference: ETIOLOGY (Hypercalcemia): "Disorders that directly increase calcium mobilization from bone, such as thyrotoxicosis, Paget's disease of bone, or osteolytic metastases, also lead to hypercalcemia with suppressed PTH secretion, as does exogenous calcium overload. The latter is most commonly observed in excessive oral ingestion (e.g., milk-alkali syndrome) or during total parenteral nutrition with excessive calcium supplementation. Ketogenic diets used to mitigate refractory seizures in children also increase bone resorption and can cause hypercalcemia. Hypercalcemia associated with prolonged immobilization arises from reduced mechanical loading of bone that increases osteocyte RANKL production and osteoclast-mediated bone resorption. In this setting, particularly with older patients, a previously unrecognized contributor to bone resorption (e.g., pHPT, Paget's disease, or myeloma) may be present and should be considered."

items.title: Symptoms of hypercalcemia range from asymptomatic at mild levels to lethargy, coma, and GI crisis at severe levels
items.content:
Mild hypercalcemia (10.5-11.9 mg/dL) is largely asymptomatic and is usually discovered only on routine calcium measurements, although some patients report vague neuropsychiatric symptoms such as difficulty concentrating, personality change, or depression.
Other common presenting features at mild-to-moderate levels include fatigue, proximal muscle weakness, constipation, hyporeflexia, nephrolithiasis, and reduced bone mineral density.
Moderate (>=12-14 mg/dL) to severe (>=14 mg/dL) hypercalcemia can produce progressive lethargy, stupor, or coma, along with prominent gastrointestinal symptoms (nausea, vomiting, anorexia, peptic ulcer, or pancreatitis) and acute renal failure.
keyPoints: Mild hypercalcemia (10.5-11.9 mg/dL) is usually asymptomatic, found incidentally
Vague neuropsychiatric symptoms (concentration difficulty, personality change, depression) can occur even at mild levels
Fatigue, proximal weakness, constipation, hyporeflexia, and nephrolithiasis are common presenting features
Moderate-to-severe hypercalcemia (>=12 mg/dL) can cause lethargy, stupor, coma, GI symptoms, and acute renal failure
items.reference: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (Hypercalcemia): "Mild hypercalcemia (10.5-11.9 mg/dL) is largely asymptomatic and usually recognized only on routine calcium measurements. Some patients may complain of vague neuropsychiatric symptoms, including trouble concentrating, personality changes, or depression. Other common presenting symptoms may include fatigue, proximal muscle weakness, constipation, hyporeflexia, or nephrolithiasis, and bone mineral density may be reduced. Moderate (>=12-14 mg/dL) to severe (>=14 mg/dL) hypercalcemia may result in progressive lethargy, stupor, or coma, as well as prominent gastrointestinal symptoms (nausea, vomiting, anorexia, peptic ulcer, or pancreatitis) and acute renal failure."

items.title: Why hypercalcemia causes polyuria, volume depletion, and prerenal failure
items.content:
Hypercalcemia impairs the kidney's urinary concentrating ability, in part by down-regulating aquaporin-2 in the renal collecting duct.
Loss of AQP2-mediated water reabsorption produces polyuria, which in turn leads to volume depletion.
The resulting hypovolemia frequently causes prerenal failure, compounding the direct renal effects of hypercalcemia and creating a cycle in which dehydration further raises serum calcium.
keyPoints: Hypercalcemia down-regulates aquaporin-2 in the collecting duct
This impairs urinary concentrating ability and causes polyuria
Polyuria leads to volume depletion
Volume depletion frequently causes prerenal failure, which can worsen hypercalcemia further
items.reference: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (Hypercalcemia): "Hypercalcemia decreases renal concentrating ability in part by down-regulating renal collecting duct aquaporin 2, which causes polyuria and volume depletion with frequent prerenal failure."

items.title: Correcting serum calcium for albumin is the first step in evaluating an abnormal calcium level
items.content:
The first step in evaluating hypercalcemia or hypocalcemia is to confirm that the abnormal calcium is not simply due to an abnormal albumin concentration (pseudohypercalcemia or pseudohypocalcemia).
About 50% of total serum calcium is ionized, with the remainder bound mainly to albumin, so it is generally preferable to measure total calcium plus albumin and calculate a corrected value rather than rely on directly measured ionized calcium, which is easily altered by collection technique.
When albumin is below the 4.0 g/dL reference value, 0.8 mg/dL is added to the total calcium for each 1.0 g/dL the albumin is below 4.0; when albumin is elevated, 0.8 mg/dL is subtracted for each 1.0 g/dL above 4.0.
keyPoints: The first diagnostic step is to rule out pseudohypercalcemia/pseudohypocalcemia from an abnormal albumin
About half of total serum calcium is ionized; the rest is mainly albumin-bound
Total calcium plus albumin is generally preferred over directly measured ionized calcium
Add 0.8 mg/dL to total calcium for each 1.0 g/dL albumin is below 4.0 g/dL (subtract if albumin is above 4.0)
items.reference: DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (Hypercalcemia): "The first step in the diagnostic evaluation of hypercalcemia or hypo-calcemia is to ensure that the reported alteration in serum calcium is not due to an abnormal albumin concentration or a similar confounder (pseudohypercalcemia or pseudohypocalcemia; Tables 57-1 and 57-2). About 50% of total serum calcium is ionized, with the remainder bound principally to albumin... When serum albumin concentrations are reduced, a corrected calcium concentration is calculated by adding 0.8 mg/dL to the total calcium level for every decrement in serum albumin of 1.0 g/dL below the albumin reference value of 4.0 g/dL. Conversely, when albumin concentrations are increased, calcium is corrected by subtracting 0.8 mg/dL from the total serum calcium for every increase in serum albumin of 1.0 g/dL above 4.0 g/dL."

items.title: How the chronicity and tempo of hypercalcemia narrows the differential diagnosis
items.content:
A detailed history provides important etiologic clues. Chronic hypercalcemia, evolving over many months to years and often fluctuating between high-normal and just-above-normal, is most commonly due to pHPT or FHH, and occasionally multiple myeloma.
Because PTH has phosphaturic effects, the hypercalcemia of pHPT is often accompanied by low or frankly low serum phosphate.
Most malignancy-associated hypercalcemia, in contrast, presents with a more acute course, and the history should also cover medications (thiazides, aromatase inhibitors), supplements, prior neck surgery, and symptoms suggestive of sarcoidosis, lymphoma, or hyperthyroidism.
keyPoints: Chronic, fluctuating hypercalcemia over months to years suggests pHPT or FHH (occasionally myeloma)
pHPT-related hypercalcemia is often accompanied by low or frankly low serum phosphate due to PTH's phosphaturic effect
Malignancy-associated hypercalcemia usually has a more acute course
History should also review medications (thiazides, aromatase inhibitors), supplements, neck surgery, and systemic symptoms
items.reference: DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (Hypercalcemia): "A detailed history provides important clues regarding the etiology of hypercalcemia (Table 57-1). Chronic hypercalcemia (many months to several years), often fluctuating between high normal to just above the limits of normal, is most commonly caused by pHPT or by FHH, and occasionally by multiple myeloma. Because of the phosphaturic actions of PTH, the hypercalcemia of pHPT is often accompanied by low serum phosphate or frank hypophosphatemia. Most malignancy-associated hypercalcemia presents with a more acute course."

items.title: An elevated or inappropriately normal PTH points to pHPT, while a suppressed PTH points to non-parathyroid causes
items.content:
Once true hypercalcemia is confirmed, the key next test is an intact PTH level by a two-site assay, along with serum creatinine to assess renal function.
If PTH is elevated or 'inappropriately normal' in the setting of elevated calcium and low-normal phosphorus, the diagnosis is almost always pHPT — especially if the 24-hour urinary fractional excretion of calcium is elevated (>0.02).
If PTH is suppressed in the face of hypercalcemia, the hypercalcemia is independent of parathyroid function and, after excluding many other causes, is most often due to an underlying malignancy.
keyPoints: After confirming true hypercalcemia, measure intact PTH (two-site assay) and serum creatinine
Elevated or inappropriately normal PTH with elevated calcium and low-normal phosphate is almost always pHPT
A fractional excretion of calcium >0.02 supports pHPT
Suppressed PTH with hypercalcemia is most often due to malignancy, after other causes are excluded
items.reference: DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (Hypercalcemia): "Once true hypercalcemia is established, the second most important laboratory test in the diagnostic evaluation is a PTH level using a two-site assay for the intact hormone... if the PTH level is increased (or \"inappropriately normal\") in the setting of elevated calcium and low-normal phosphorus, the diagnosis is almost always pHPT, particularly if the 24-hour urinary fractional excretion of calcium is elevated (>0.02; described below)... A suppressed PTH level in the face of hypercalcemia indicates that the hypercalcemia is independent of parathyroid gland function; while many other causes must be excluded (Table 57-1), this is most often due to an underlying malignancy."

items.title: Why a calcium/creatinine clearance ratio below 0.01 suggests FHH rather than pHPT
items.content:
Because FHH impairs CaSR function in both the parathyroid glands and the kidney, urinary calcium excretion is inappropriately low relative to the degree of hypercalcemia.
A calcium/creatinine clearance ratio — urine calcium/serum calcium divided by urine creatinine/serum creatinine — below 0.01 is suggestive of FHH, especially with a family history of mild asymptomatic hypercalcemia or repeated unsuccessful parathyroid surgery for presumed pHPT.
When urine calcium studies are inconclusive, sequencing of the CaSR-signaling genes CASR, GNA11, and AP2S1 is now commonly used to make a definitive diagnosis of FHH.
keyPoints: FHH impairs CaSR function in both parathyroid and kidney, causing inappropriately low urinary calcium excretion
Calcium/creatinine clearance ratio = (urine Ca/serum Ca) / (urine creatinine/serum creatinine)
A ratio <0.01 is suggestive of FHH, especially with a relevant family history or failed parathyroid surgery
CASR, GNA11, and AP2S1 sequencing can give a definitive diagnosis when urine studies are inconclusive
items.reference: DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (Hypercalcemia): "Due to impaired CaSR function in parathyroids and kidney, a calcium/creatinine clearance ratio (calculated as urine calcium/serum calcium divided by urine creatinine/serum creatinine) of <0.01 is suggestive of FHH, particularly when there is a family history of mild, asymptomatic hypercalcemia or recurrent unsuccessful surgeries for \"presumed\" pHPT. Sequencing of the genes that control CaSR signaling (CASR, GNA11, AP2S1) is now commonly performed for the definitive diagnosis of FHH, particularly if urine calcium studies are inconclusive."

items.title: How PTHrP and 1,25(OH)2D measurements distinguish malignancy from granulomatous/lymphoma causes when PTH is suppressed
items.content:
When PTH is suppressed in a hypercalcemic patient, a PTHrP level may be needed to confirm hypercalcemia of malignancy, even though the causative tumor is usually overt.
Multiple myeloma should also be evaluated, including serum free light chains, since standard electrophoresis with immunofixation can miss ~3% of myeloma.
Hypercalcemia is one of the few settings where serum 1,25(OH)2D is clinically useful: in granulomatous disorders or lymphoma, 1,25(OH)2D is frankly increased or inappropriately normal despite a low PTH, whereas serum 25(OH)D is useful for evaluating both hypercalcemia and hypocalcemia.
keyPoints: A PTHrP level may be needed to establish hypercalcemia of malignancy when PTH is suppressed
Serum free light chains should be checked for myeloma even if electrophoresis/immunofixation is negative
1,25(OH)2D is frankly increased or inappropriately normal in granulomatous disease/lymphoma despite low PTH
Serum 25(OH)D is useful in evaluating both hypercalcemia and hypocalcemia
items.reference: DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (Hypercalcemia): "Although a tumor that causes hypercalcemia is generally overt, a PTHrP level may be needed to establish the diagnosis of hypercalcemia of malignancy. Multiple myeloma must be evaluated in this setting as well, including measurement of serum free light chains since ~3% of myeloma may be undetected by serum or urine protein electrophoresis with immunofixation. Measurement of serum 25(OH)D is useful for evaluation of both hypercalcemia and hypocalcemia. Hypercalcemia is one of the few settings where a serum 1,25(OH)2D measurement is useful. In hypercalcemia caused by granulomatous disorders or lymphomas, serum 1,25(OH)2D levels are either frankly increased or inappropriately normal, in the setting of a low serum PTH."

items.title: VITAMINS TRAP is a mnemonic for the broad differential diagnosis of hypercalcemia
items.content:
Clinical evaluation combined with laboratory testing usually provides a diagnosis of hypercalcemia in the vast majority of cases.
The mnemonic VITAMINS TRAP captures much of the differential: vitamin D/vitamin A intoxication; immobilization; thyrotoxicosis, thiazides, 24-hydroxylase (CYP24A1) deficiency; Addison's disease, acromegaly, acquired autoimmune disease; milk-alkali syndrome; chronic inflammation; neoplasia (including multiple myeloma); sarcoidosis; tuberculosis or other granulomatous disease; rhabdomyolysis recovery or denosumab-discontinuation rebound; AIDS; and PTH/Paget's/parenteral sources/pheochromocytoma/pregnancy-associated PTHrP/pseudohypercalcemia.
This mnemonic is a useful checklist once the more common causes (pHPT, malignancy, FHH, granulomatous disease, vitamin D-related) have been considered.
keyPoints: Clinical evaluation plus laboratory testing usually yields a diagnosis of hypercalcemia
VITAMINS TRAP is a mnemonic covering the broad differential of hypercalcemia
It spans endocrine (thyrotoxicosis, Addison's, acromegaly), drug (thiazides, milk-alkali), and neoplastic/granulomatous causes
It is most useful as a checklist after the most common causes have been excluded
items.reference: DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (Hypercalcemia): "In the vast majority of cases, clinical evaluation in combination with laboratory testing should provide a diagnosis. The very useful mnemonic \"VITAMINS TRAP\" captures much of the differential for the various disorders listed in Table 57-1: vitamin D, vitamin A intoxication; immobilization; thyrotoxicosis, thiazides, 24-hydroxylase deficiency; Addison's disease, acromegaly, acquired autoimmune; milk-alkali syndrome; inflammation (chronic); neoplasia including multiple myeloma; sarcoidosis; tuberculosis or another granulomatous disease; rhabdomyolysis recovery phase, rebound from denosumab discontinuation; AIDS; PTH (hyperparathyroidism), Paget's disease, parenteral sources, pheochromocytoma, pregnancy-associated PTHrP, and pseudohypercalcemia."

items.subtopic: Treatment of Severe Hypercalcemia — Hydration and Bisphosphonates
items.title: Volume expansion followed by IV aminobisphosphonates is the cornerstone of treating significant hypercalcemia
items.content:
Mild, asymptomatic hypercalcemia does not need immediate therapy beyond adequate oral hydration and follow-up directed at the underlying cause.
Significant, symptomatic hypercalcemia requires acute correction regardless of etiology, beginning with volume expansion — often 4-6 L of IV saline over the first 24 hours, adjusted for comorbidities such as heart failure — because hypercalcemia invariably causes dehydration. Saline improves renal perfusion and natriuresis, which increases calciuresis; loop diuretics can augment this but must not be started until volume status is normalized.
Following hydration, intravenous aminobisphosphonates (potent inhibitors of osteoclast-mediated bone resorption) are the mainstay of therapy for the hypercalcemia of malignancy and parathyroid crisis.
keyPoints: Mild asymptomatic hypercalcemia needs only hydration and follow-up directed at the cause
Significant symptomatic hypercalcemia requires acute correction, starting with 4-6 L IV saline over 24 h
Saline improves renal perfusion/natriuresis and thus calciuresis; loop diuretics should not be started until volume is restored
IV aminobisphosphonates are the mainstay following hydration for hypercalcemia of malignancy and parathyroid crisis
items.reference: TREATMENT — Hypercalcemia: "Mild, asymptomatic hypercalcemia does not require immediate therapy beyond assiduous maintenance of adequate oral hydration with close diagnostic follow-up; management is dictated by the underlying cause. By contrast, significant, symptomatic hypercalcemia requires acute intervention to restore normocalcemia independent of the etiology, in addition to treatment of the underlying cause. Initial therapy of significant hypercalcemia begins with volume expansion because hypercalcemia invariably leads to dehydration, as noted above; 4-6 L of intravenous saline may be required over the first 24 h... Although loop diuretics can be transiently used to further augment sodium and thus calcium excretion by the distal tubule, loop diuretics should not be initiated until the volume status has been restored to normal. Following intravenous hydration, which helps to most rapidly address hypercalcemia, the mainstays of therapy are intravenous aminobisphosphonates."

items.subtopic: Calcitonin, Denosumab, and Cinacalcet in Hypercalcemia
items.title: How calcitonin, denosumab, and cinacalcet supplement bisphosphonates in hypercalcemia management
items.content:
Aminobisphosphonates such as zoledronic acid and pamidronate normalize serum calcium in 60-90% of patients within 1-2 days.
Calcitonin (4-8 IU/kg IM or SC every 6 h) gives a more rapid, though transient, reduction in bone resorption and is routinely used during the first 48 hours alongside hydration and bisphosphonate infusion. Denosumab, a RANKL antibody, is effective for hypercalcemia of malignancy refractory to bisphosphonates; lower doses (e.g., 0.3 mg/kg) can be used in advanced kidney disease because aminobisphosphonates are contraindicated below a GFR of 35 mL/min/1.73 m^2 while denosumab is cleared by the reticuloendothelial system.
Parathyroid carcinoma causing hypercalcemia of malignancy is treated with the CaSR agonist cinacalcet (starting at 30 mg orally twice daily, titrated to normalize calcium), which is also used in pHPT patients who are not surgical candidates; dialysis may be needed for severe hypercalcemia with anuric renal failure or heart failure.
keyPoints: IV aminobisphosphonates normalize calcium in 60-90% of patients within 1-2 days
Calcitonin gives rapid but transient bone-resorption reduction during the first 48 hours
Denosumab is effective in bisphosphonate-refractory hypercalcemia of malignancy and is preferred (at lower dose) in advanced kidney disease
Cinacalcet (CaSR agonist) treats parathyroid carcinoma-related hypercalcemia and pHPT in non-surgical candidates
Dialysis may be required for severe hypercalcemia with anuric renal failure or heart failure
items.reference: TREATMENT — Hypercalcemia: "Zoledronic acid (4 mg intravenously over ~30 min) and pamidronate (60-90 mg intravenously over 2-4 h) are commonly used for the treatment of hypercalcemia of malignancy and parathyroid crisis in adults... The onset of intravenous aminobisphosphonate action is within 1-2 days, with normalization of serum calcium levels occurring in 60-90% of patients. To achieve a more rapid albeit transient reduction in bone resorption, calcitonin is routinely implemented as well (4-8 IU/kg given intramuscularly or subcutaneously every 6 h) during the first 48 hours following admission in conjunction with hydration and aminobisphosphonate infusion. Denosumab... an antibody to RANKL, is a potent inhibitor of bone resorption and has been shown to be effective in treating hypercalcemia of malignancy refractory to aminobisphosphonates. Lower doses of denosumab (e.g., 0.3 mg/kg) can be used to treat hypercalcemia patients in the setting of advanced kidney disease, since intravenous aminobisphosphonates are contraindicated with glomerular filtration rate <35 mL/min/1.73 m^2 and denosumab is cleared by the reticuloendothelial system. In some instances, dialysis may be required to control severe hypercalcemia, particularly in the settings of anuric acute renal failure or congestive heart failure. When diagnosed, parathyroid carcinoma, a rare cause of hypercalcemia of malignancy driven by PTH, is treated with the CaSR agonist cinacalcet, starting with 30 mg orally twice daily and titrated to normalize serum calcium. Of note, cinacalcet is also used to treat patients with pHPT who are not candidates for parathyroid gland surgery."

items.title: Why glucocorticoids are foundational therapy for vitamin D- or 1,25(OH)2D-mediated hypercalcemia
items.content:
In hypercalcemia driven by vitamin D or 1,25(OH)2D (e.g., granulomatous disease, vitamin D intoxication), glucocorticoids are foundational because they directly reduce intestinal calcium absorption and decrease 1,25(OH)2D production.
Intravenous hydrocortisone (200-400 mg/d) for 3-5 days or oral prednisone (40-60 mg/d) for 7-10 days is most often used, with a prolonged taper guided by serum calcium.
For severe vitamin D intoxication requiring hospitalization, IV hydration and aminobisphosphonates are often still needed to rapidly restore normocalcemia, and for thyroid-storm-related hypercalcemia, aggressive treatment of the thyrotoxicosis (including IV glucocorticoids and thionamides) usually normalizes calcium.
keyPoints: Glucocorticoids reduce intestinal calcium absorption and 1,25(OH)2D production
IV hydrocortisone (200-400 mg/d) x 3-5 days or oral prednisone (40-60 mg/d) x 7-10 days, then prolonged taper, is typical
Severe vitamin D intoxication may still need IV hydration plus aminobisphosphonates
Thyroid-storm hypercalcemia usually resolves with aggressive treatment of the thyrotoxicosis itself
items.reference: TREATMENT — Hypercalcemia: "In patients with vitamin D- or 1,25(OH)2D-mediated hypercalcemia, glucocorticoids represent foundational pharmacotherapy, as they directly reduce calcium absorption from the small intestine and decrease 1,25(OH)2D production. Intravenous hydrocortisone (200-400 mg daily) for 3-5 days or oral prednisone (40-60 mg daily) for 7-10 days is used most often, with prolonged taper as guided by serum calcium. However, for severe vitamin D-induced intoxication requiring hospitalization, intravenous hydration and aminobisphosphonates are frequently necessary to rapidly restore normocalcemia... For hypercalcemia of thyroid storm, the aggressive management of the underlying thyrotoxicosis, which also includes intravenous glucocorticoid administration in addition to thionamides, usually restores normocalcemia."

items.title: Hypocalcemia is differentiated by whether PTH is low (hypoparathyroidism) or appropriately high (secondary hyperparathyroidism)
items.content:
The causes of hypocalcemia are differentiated by whether serum PTH is low (hypoparathyroidism) or high (secondary hyperparathyroidism); impaired PTH production and severe impairment of intestinal calcium absorption (with or without vitamin D deficiency) are the most common etiologies.
Because PTH is the main minute-to-minute defense against hypocalcemia, disorders of deficient PTH production can cause profound, debilitating, and life-threatening hypocalcemia.
In adults, hypoparathyroidism most often results from inadvertent damage to all four parathyroid glands during thyroid or parathyroid surgery, and autoimmune endocrinopathies account for about a quarter of hypoparathyroidism cases.
keyPoints: Hypocalcemia is classified by whether PTH is low (hypoparathyroidism) or appropriately elevated (secondary hyperparathyroidism)
Impaired PTH production and severe intestinal calcium malabsorption are the most common etiologies
PTH deficiency can cause profound, life-threatening hypocalcemia because PTH is the main acute defense against it
Neck surgery causing inadvertent damage to all four parathyroid glands is the most common adult cause of hypoparathyroidism
Autoimmune endocrinopathies account for about a quarter of hypoparathyroidism
items.reference: HYPOCALCEMIA — ETIOLOGY: "The causes of hypocalcemia can be differentiated according to whether serum PTH levels are low (hypoparathyroidism) or high (secondary hyperparathyroidism). Although there are many potential causes of hypocalcemia, impaired PTH production and severely impaired intestinal calcium absorption with or without profound vitamin D deficiency are the most common etiologies (Table 57-2) (Chap. 422). Because PTH is the main minute-by-minute defense against hypocalcemia, disorders associated with deficient PTH production may be associated with profound, debilitating, and life-threatening hypocalcemia. In adults, hypoparathyroidism most commonly results from inadvertent damage to all four parathyroid glands during thyroid or parathyroid gland surgery. Hypoparathyroidism is a cardinal feature of autoimmune endocrinopathies (Chap. 400), responsible for about a quarter of hypoparathyroidism."

items.title: How magnesium deficiency causes hypocalcemia by impairing PTH secretion
items.content:
Beyond surgical and autoimmune causes, impaired PTH secretion is commonly caused by magnesium deficiency acting through intracellular mechanisms.
Hypomagnesemia with hypocalcemia can result from drug-induced renal magnesium loss — chronic alcohol abuse, cisplatin or anti-EGF antibody chemotherapy, proton pump inhibitors — or from rare autoimmune tubulointerstitial nephritis with claudin-16 inhibition.
Conversely, severe hypermagnesemia (e.g., from obstetric magnesium infusions for preeclampsia or preterm labor) can also suppress PTH release, because extracellular magnesium binds the CaSR with roughly one-third the affinity of calcium, occasionally causing symptomatic hypoparathyroidism that resolves once the infusion is stopped.
keyPoints: Magnesium deficiency impairs PTH secretion via intracellular actions, causing hypocalcemia
Causes of hypomagnesemia-related hypocalcemia include alcohol abuse, cisplatin/anti-EGF antibodies, and PPIs
Rare autoimmune tubulointerstitial nephritis with claudin-16 inhibition can also cause renal magnesium wasting
Severe hypermagnesemia (e.g., obstetric magnesium infusions) can also suppress PTH via the CaSR, causing transient hypoparathyroidism
items.reference: HYPOCALCEMIA — ETIOLOGY: "More commonly, impaired PTH secretion may be secondary to magnesium deficiency (via intracellular actions). Hypomagnesemia with hypocalcemia can arise from drug-induced renal loss including chronic alcohol abuse, oncotherapeutics (cisplatin, anti-epidermal growth factor [EGF] antibodies), proton pump inhibitors, or rare autoimmunity that causes tubulointerstitial nephritis with claudin-16 inhibition. Because extracellular magnesium can bind the CaSR with an affinity about one-third that of calcium, severe hypermagnesemia arising from obstetric magnesium infusions for preeclampsia or preterm labor can also suppress PTH release; this rarely causes symptomatic hypoparathyroidism that must be managed until the magnesium infusion is discontinued."

items.title: Why hypocalcemia from vitamin D deficiency or CKD is usually less severe than from hypoparathyroidism
items.content:
Vitamin D deficiency, impaired 1,25(OH)2D production from chronic renal insufficiency, and vitamin D resistance all cause hypocalcemia.
However, the hypocalcemia in these disorders is generally less severe than in hypoparathyroidism, because intact parathyroid glands can mount a compensatory increase in PTH secretion that partially buffers the fall in serum calcium — at the cost of increased bone resorption.
Alkalosis (respiratory, e.g., hyperventilation, or metabolic, e.g., intractable vomiting) can also cause hypocalcemia by increasing calcium binding to albumin; when tetany occurs with alkalosis, another cause of impaired calcium homeostasis is usually also present. Severe tissue injury (burns, rhabdomyolysis, tumor lysis, pancreatitis) can cause hypocalcemia through a combination of hyperphosphatemia, tissue calcium deposition, and impaired PTH secretion.
keyPoints: Vitamin D deficiency, CKD-related impaired 1,25(OH)2D production, and vitamin D resistance cause hypocalcemia
These are usually less severe than hypoparathyroidism because intact parathyroids can compensate with increased PTH
This compensation comes 'to the detriment of bone' via increased resorption
Alkalosis increases calcium-albumin binding and can cause hypocalcemia; tetany with alkalosis suggests another coexisting cause
Severe tissue injury (burns, rhabdomyolysis, tumor lysis, pancreatitis) can cause hypocalcemia via hyperphosphatemia, tissue calcium deposition, and impaired PTH
items.reference: HYPOCALCEMIA — ETIOLOGY: "Vitamin D deficiency, impaired 1,25(OH)2D production due to chronic renal insufficiency, or vitamin D resistance also cause hypocalcemia. However, the degree of hypocalcemia in these disorders is most often not as severe as that seen with hypoparathyroidism; this is because the parathyroids are capable of mounting a compensatory increase in PTH secretion that buffers the extent of hypocalcemia to the detriment of bone. By increasing calcium binding to albumin, both respiratory alkalosis (e.g., hyperventilation) and metabolic alkalosis (e.g., intractable vomiting) can also cause hypocalcemia. When tetany arises with alkalosis, another cause of impaired calcium homeostasis is frequently present. Hypocalcemia may also occur in conditions associated with severe tissue injury such as burns, rhabdomyolysis, tumor lysis, or pancreatitis."

items.title: Hypocalcemia produces neuromuscular irritability ranging from paresthesias to tetany, seizures, and QTc prolongation
items.content:
Hypocalcemia may be asymptomatic if mild and chronic, or it may present with life-threatening complications.
Moderate-to-severe hypocalcemia causes paresthesias of the fingers, toes, and circumoral region from increased neuromuscular irritability. Chvostek's sign (twitching of the perioral/zygomatic muscles on tapping the facial nerve anterior to the ear) may be present but also occurs in ~10% of normal individuals; Trousseau's sign (carpal spasm after 3 minutes of blood-pressure-cuff inflation above systolic pressure, with ipsilateral thumb apposition and wrist flexion) is more definitive for impending tetany.
Profound vitamin D insufficiency or hypomagnesemia often produces proximal myopathy, and severe hypocalcemia can cause seizures, carpopedal spasm, bronchospasm, laryngospasm, and QTc prolongation with risk of Torsade de pointes and sudden death.
keyPoints: Hypocalcemia can be asymptomatic if mild/chronic or life-threatening if severe
Paresthesias of fingers, toes, and circumoral region reflect increased neuromuscular irritability
Chvostek's sign is present in ~10% of normal individuals; Trousseau's sign (carpal spasm with cuff inflation) is more definitive
Proximal myopathy can occur with profound vitamin D insufficiency or hypomagnesemia
Severe hypocalcemia can cause seizures, laryngospasm, QTc prolongation, Torsade de pointes, and sudden death
items.reference: HYPOCALCEMIA — CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS: "Patients with hypocalcemia may be asymptomatic if the decreases in serum calcium are relatively mild and chronic, or they may present with life-threatening complications. Moderate to severe hypocalcemia is associated with paresthesias, usually of the fingers, toes, and circum-oral regions, and is caused by increased neuromuscular irritability. On physical examination, a Chvostek's sign... may be elicited, although it is also present in ~10% of normal individuals. As a more definitive sign of neuromuscular irritability and impending tetany, carpal spasm may be induced by inflation of an upper arm blood pressure cuff to 20 mmHg above the patient's systolic blood pressure for 3 min (Trousseau's sign; ipsilateral thumb apposition with wrist flexion). With profound vitamin D insufficiency or hypomagnesemia, proximal myopathy is frequently evident... Severe hypocalcemia can induce seizures, carpopedal spasm, bronchospasm, laryngospasm, and prolongation of the QTc interval with risk of ventricular tachyarrhythmia (cardiac Torsade) and sudden death."

items.title: PTH level distinguishes hypoparathyroidism from secondary hyperparathyroidism as the cause of hypocalcemia
items.content:
Evaluation of hypocalcemia should include albumin, phosphorus, and magnesium in addition to calcium, with PTH again central to the workup.
A suppressed or inappropriately low PTH establishes hypoparathyroidism as the cause, and the history (e.g., prior neck surgery) often reveals the underlying mechanism.
An elevated PTH (secondary hyperparathyroidism, a compensatory response to low calcium) instead points to the vitamin D axis or primary intestinal disease (e.g., short bowel syndrome after Roux-en-Y bariatric surgery); serum 25(OH)D assesses nutritional vitamin D status, 24-hour urinary calcium is low in both vitamin D deficiency and intestinal calcium malabsorption, and 1,25(OH)2D is informative when vitamin D resistance (nonnutritional rickets) is suspected.
keyPoints: Workup includes albumin, phosphorus, magnesium, and PTH alongside calcium
Suppressed/inappropriately low PTH establishes hypoparathyroidism as the cause
Elevated PTH (secondary hyperparathyroidism) points to the vitamin D axis or primary intestinal disease
Serum 25(OH)D reflects vitamin D stores; 24-h urine calcium is low in vitamin D deficiency and intestinal malabsorption
1,25(OH)2D is informative when vitamin D resistance/nonnutritional rickets is suspected
items.reference: HYPOCALCEMIA — DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH: "In addition to measuring serum calcium, it is important to determine albumin, phosphorus, and magnesium levels. As for the evaluation of hypercalcemia, determining the PTH level is central to the evaluation of hypocalcemia. A suppressed (or \"inappropriately low\") PTH level in the setting of hypocalcemia establishes impaired PTH secretion (hypoparathyroidism) as the cause of the hypocalcemia. The history will often elicit the underlying cause (e.g., prior neck surgery with parathyroid gland destruction). By contrast, an elevated PTH level (secondary hyperparathyroidism, a compensatory change in response to low calcium) should direct attention to the vitamin D axis or primary disease of the small intestine (e.g., short bowel syndrome due to Roux-en-Y bariatric surgery) as the cause of hypocalcemia."

items.title: IV calcium gluconate with telemetry is the initial treatment for acute symptomatic hypocalcemia
items.content:
Treatment of hypocalcemia depends on its severity, the speed of onset, and accompanying complications such as tetany, seizures, or QTc prolongation.
Acute symptomatic hypocalcemia is initially managed with a 10-mL ampule of 10% calcium gluconate (90 mg or 2.2 mmol) diluted in 50 mL of 5% dextrose or 0.9% saline, given IV over 5-10 minutes with telemetry; one or two further ampules may be given at 10-60 minute intervals as needed.
While transitioning to oral therapy, a continuous IV calcium infusion (often starting at 1 mg/min elemental calcium in adults) titrated to symptoms, ECG, and calcium levels is often required, with calcium checked every 4-6 hours; coexisting hypomagnesemia should be corrected, and chronic therapy started only once tetany has resolved, the patient is stable, and the QTc has normalized.
keyPoints: Treatment depends on severity, rapidity of onset, and complications (tetany, seizures, QTc prolongation)
Acute symptomatic hypocalcemia: IV calcium gluconate (10 mL of 10%, diluted) over 5-10 min with telemetry, repeated as needed
A continuous IV calcium infusion (~1 mg/min elemental calcium) may be needed while transitioning to oral therapy
Coexisting hypomagnesemia should be corrected; start chronic therapy only after tetany resolves and QTc normalizes
items.reference: HYPOCALCEMIA — TREATMENT: "Acute, symptomatic hypocalcemia is initially managed with calcium gluconate, a 10-mL ampule of 10% wt/vol (90 mg or 2.2 mmol) diluted in 50 mL of 5% dextrose or 0.9% sodium chloride and given intravenously over 5-10 min with telemetry. After one or two additional ampules are given at 10-60 minute intervals as needed to initially resolve symptoms, while transitioning to oral medication symptomatic hypocalcemia often requires a period of continuous intravenous calcium infusion (often starting at 1 mg/min elemental calcium for adults) that is titrated to symptoms, ECG, and blood calcium levels targeting the lower limit of normal. Telemetry with serial exams and blood calcium levels every 4-6 hours should be closely monitored. Accompanying hypomagnesemia, if present, should be treated with appropriate magnesium supplementation, with initiation of chronic-phase treatment once tetany has resolved, the patient is stable and able to safely take oral medication, and the QTc has normalized."

items.title: Why normalizing serum calcium without PTH replacement risks hypercalciuria and nephrocalcinosis in hypoparathyroidism
items.content:
Chronic hypocalcemia from hypoparathyroidism is treated with oral calcium (1000-3000 mg/d elemental calcium in divided doses) plus carefully titrated oral calcitriol (0.25-1 ug/d), aiming for an albumin-corrected low-normal serum calcium.
Distal convoluted tubule calcium reabsorption (about 10% of the filtered load) is PTH-dependent, so raising serum calcium above the low-normal range without PTH replacement increases urinary calcium excretion, raising the risk of nephrolithiasis and nephrocalcinosis.
24-hour urine calcium targets are therefore kept under 250 mg in women and 300 mg in men, and adequate vitamin D3 nutrition (1000-2000 IU/d) should still be maintained and monitored every 6-12 months via 25(OH)D levels.
keyPoints: Chronic hypoparathyroidism: oral calcium 1000-3000 mg/d plus titrated calcitriol 0.25-1 ug/d, targeting low-normal corrected calcium
Distal tubule calcium reabsorption is PTH-dependent (~10% of filtered load)
Without PTH replacement, raising calcium above low-normal increases hypercalciuria, nephrolithiasis, and nephrocalcinosis risk
24-h urine calcium targets: <250 mg (women), <300 mg (men)
Vitamin D3 nutrition (1000-2000 IU/d) should still be maintained and monitored with 25(OH)D every 6-12 months
items.reference: HYPOCALCEMIA — TREATMENT: "Chronic hypocalcemia due to hypoparathyroidism is treated with oral calcium supplements (1000-3000 mg/d elemental calcium in divided doses), with careful titration of oral calcitriol [1,25(OH)2D, 0.25-1 ug/d] to achieve albumin-corrected low-normal serum calcium levels. Because calcium resorption in the distal convoluted tubule (about 10% of the filtered load) is dependent upon PTH, normalization of calcium to above the low-normal range in the absence of PTH replacement increases hypercalciuria with risk of nephrolithiasis and nephrocalcinosis. Urine 24-hour calcium targets are under 250 mg (women) to 300 mg (men). Adequate vitamin D3 nutrition (1000-2000 IU daily for most adults) must still be maintained and monitored every 6-12 months, measuring serum 25(OH)D levels."

items.subtopic: Chronic Treatment of Hypoparathyroidism — PTH Replacement and Vitamin D Deficiency
items.title: PTH (1-34) replacement and tailored vitamin D dosing complete the chronic management of hypocalcemia
items.content:
PTH (1-84) (Natpara) was FDA-approved for hypoparathyroidism but has now been discontinued by the manufacturer. Teriparatide (PTH 1-34), approved for osteoporosis, has been used off-label (20 ug SC twice daily) for refractory hypoparathyroidism, with dose reduction in chronic kidney disease to avoid hypercalcemia; a pegylated prodrug (palopegteriparatide) enabling once-daily dosing is under regulatory consideration.
With PTH replacement, routine vitamin D3 (~1000 IU/d) and calcium (~1000 mg/d) suffice and hypercalciuria should be absent.
Hypocalcemia from vitamin D deficiency is treated with vitamin D supplementation scaled to severity: nutritional deficiency typically responds to ergocalciferol 50,000 IU 2-3 times weekly for several months, whereas severe enteric malabsorption (e.g., after Roux-en-Y bariatric surgery) may require ergocalciferol titrated up to 100,000 IU/d, with calcium citrate preferred in intestinal disease because it is better absorbed and increases urinary citrate to mitigate nephrolithiasis risk from increased oxalate absorption.
keyPoints: PTH (1-84)/Natpara is discontinued; teriparatide (PTH 1-34) is used off-label for refractory hypoparathyroidism (20 ug SC twice daily)
Teriparatide dose may need reduction in CKD to avoid hypercalcemia; palopegteriparatide (once-daily) is under regulatory review
With PTH replacement, routine vitamin D3 (~1000 IU/d) and calcium (~1000 mg/d) suffice without hypercalciuria
Nutritional vitamin D deficiency: ergocalciferol 50,000 IU 2-3x/week for months
Severe enteric malabsorption (e.g., post-Roux-en-Y): ergocalciferol up to 100,000 IU/d, with calcium citrate preferred
items.reference: HYPOCALCEMIA — TREATMENT: "PTH (1-84) (Natpara), approved by the Food and Drug Administration for treatment of hypoparathyroidism, has now been discontinued by the manufacturer. PTH (1-34), also known as teriparatide, is approved for osteoporosis, but has been successfully used off-label (20 ug subcutaneously twice daily) for treating refractory hypoparathyroidism. Dosing may need to be decreased in patients with chronic renal insufficiency to avoid hypercalcemia. Palopegteriparatide, a pegylated prodrug form of teriparatide with pharmacokinetics that enable daily subcutaneous dosing for PTH replacement, is now under consideration for regulatory approval. Routine daily vitamin D (~1000 IU D3) and calcium (~1000 mg elemental calcium) nutrition suffices, and hypercalciuria should be absent, with PTH replacement strategies. Hypocalcemia from vitamin D deficiency is best treated using vitamin D supplementation, with the dose depending on the severity of the deficit and the underlying cause. Thus, nutritional vitamin D deficiency generally responds to moderate doses of oral vitamin D (e.g., ergocalciferol at 50,000 IU, 2-3 times per week for several months), whereas vitamin D deficiency due to severe enteric malabsorption may require much higher doses (e.g., ergocalciferol carefully titrated up to 100,000 IU/d), as is often the case following Roux-en-Y bariatric surgery. In the setting of intestinal disease, calcium supplementation as calcium citrate is preferred because it is better absorbed, and this formulation increases urinary citrate to mitigate the risk of nephrolithiasis in this setting due to increased dietary oxalate absorption."

items.question: A 58-year-old woman has an incidental serum calcium of 11.2 mg/dL on routine labs, confirmed on repeat testing. Serum phosphate is low-normal, and intact PTH (two-site assay) is mildly elevated. Which diagnosis is most likely?
options: Primary hyperparathyroidism
Hypercalcemia of malignancy
Vitamin D intoxication
Familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia
items.explanation: An elevated or inappropriately normal PTH in the setting of elevated calcium and low-normal phosphorus is almost always pHPT, especially if the fractional excretion of calcium is elevated (>0.02).
items.reference: DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (Hypercalcemia): "if the PTH level is increased (or \"inappropriately normal\") in the setting of elevated calcium and low-normal phosphorus, the diagnosis is almost always pHPT, particularly if the 24-hour urinary fractional excretion of calcium is elevated (>0.02; described below)."

items.question: A 32-year-old man has mild, lifelong hypercalcemia with a mildly elevated PTH and a family history of similarly asymptomatic hypercalcemia. A prior parathyroidectomy for presumed pHPT failed to normalize his calcium. Which finding would support a diagnosis of FHH rather than pHPT?
options: Calcium/creatinine clearance ratio <0.01
24-hour urinary fractional excretion of calcium >0.02
Elevated serum 1,25(OH)2D with suppressed PTH
Elevated PTHrP level
items.explanation: A calcium/creatinine clearance ratio below 0.01 — reflecting inappropriately low urinary calcium excretion due to impaired CaSR function in the kidney and parathyroid — is suggestive of FHH, especially with a relevant family history and failed parathyroid surgery.
items.reference: DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (Hypercalcemia): "Due to impaired CaSR function in parathyroids and kidney, a calcium/creatinine clearance ratio (calculated as urine calcium/serum calcium divided by urine creatinine/serum creatinine) of <0.01 is suggestive of FHH, particularly when there is a family history of mild, asymptomatic hypercalcemia or recurrent unsuccessful surgeries for \"presumed\" pHPT."

items.question: A 70-year-old man with squamous cell lung cancer develops hypercalcemia. Serum PTH is suppressed and serum phosphate is low. Which mediator most likely explains his hypercalcemia?
options: PTH-related peptide (PTHrP)
1,25(OH)2D from tumor CYP27B1 activity
Excess dietary calcium absorption
An inactivating CASR mutation
items.explanation: Many solid tumors, including squamous cell carcinomas, secrete PTHrP, which binds the PTH receptor and mimics PTH's actions on bone and kidney, causing hypercalcemia with appropriately suppressed endogenous PTH.
items.reference: ETIOLOGY (Hypercalcemia): "Many solid tumors produce PTH-related peptide (PTHrP). Once processed and secreted, PTHrP shares homology with the first 13 amino acids of PTH and binds the PTH receptor, thus mimicking effects of PTH on bone and kidney to cause hypercalcemia."

items.question: A 45-year-old woman with biopsy-proven sarcoidosis presents with hypercalcemia and a suppressed PTH. Which additional laboratory finding would be expected?
options: Elevated or inappropriately normal serum 1,25(OH)2D
Elevated PTHrP level
Low serum 25(OH)D with low 1,25(OH)2D
Calcium/creatinine clearance ratio <0.01 consistent with FHH
items.explanation: In granulomatous disorders, macrophage CYP27B1 converts 25(OH)D to 1,25(OH)2D, so serum 1,25(OH)2D is frankly increased or inappropriately normal despite a low PTH.
items.reference: DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (Hypercalcemia): "In hypercalcemia caused by granulomatous disorders or lymphomas, serum 1,25(OH)2D levels are either frankly increased or inappropriately normal, in the setting of a low serum PTH."

items.question: A patient who ingested a massive accidental overdose of vitamin D several weeks ago continues to have hypercalcemia despite stopping the supplement. Which statement best explains this prolonged course?
options: The long half-lives of vitamin D and 25(OH)D can prolong hypercalcemia for weeks, even though 1,25(OH)D is metabolized within a day or two
1,25(OH)2D itself has a half-life of several weeks
Vitamin D toxicity permanently abolishes CYP24A1 activity
The kidneys cannot excrete excess calcium once vitamin D levels rise
items.explanation: Although 1,25(OH)D is normally metabolized within a day or two, the much longer half-lives of vitamin D and 25(OH)D allow hypercalcemia from vitamin D intoxication to persist for several weeks.
items.reference: ETIOLOGY (Hypercalcemia): "While 1,25(OH)D is normally metabolized within a day or two, the longer half-lives of vitamin D and 25(OH)D can prolong hypercalcemia from vitamin D intoxication for several weeks."

items.question: A young adult with recurrent hypercalcemia and nephrocalcinosis is found to have a genetic deficiency of the 24-hydroxylase enzyme (CYP24A1) that normally inactivates 1,25(OH)2D. Which agents can help control the hypercalcemia in this condition?
options: Ketoconazole or fluconazole, to inhibit 1,25(OH)2D synthesis
Intravenous aminobisphosphonates as first-line monotherapy
Cinacalcet, to lower PTH secretion
Loop diuretics as initial monotherapy
items.explanation: For genetic CYP24A1 deficiency, inhibiting 1,25(OH)2D synthesis with ketoconazole or fluconazole can help control hypercalcemia and also reduces the need for chronic glucocorticoids.
items.reference: TREATMENT — Hypercalcemia: "for individuals with genetic CYP24A1 deficiency, inhibiting 1,25(OH)2D synthesis with ketoconazole or fluconazole can help control hypercalcemia and mitigate the toxicity of chronic glucocorticoid administration."

items.question: A patient on long-term lithium therapy for bipolar disorder is found to have mild hypercalcemia with a mildly elevated PTH. What is the most likely mechanism?
options: Lithium raises the CaSR set point for calcium feedback, causing parathyroid hyperplasia or adenoma
Lithium directly stimulates osteoclast RANKL production independent of PTH
Lithium causes ectopic PTH secretion from a renal tumor
Lithium impairs renal 1,25(OH)2D catabolism via CYP24A1 inhibition
items.explanation: Prolonged lithium therapy raises the CaSR set point for negative feedback by calcium, leading to parathyroid hyperplasia or adenoma and a pHPT-like picture.
items.reference: DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (Hypercalcemia): "Prolonged lithium therapy increases the CaSR set point for negative feedback by calcium and can cause hyperparathyroidism due to parathyroid hyperplasia or adenoma."

items.question: An elderly woman taking large amounts of calcium carbonate antacids for dyspepsia develops hypercalcemia, metabolic alkalosis, and renal insufficiency. Which category of the VITAMINS TRAP differential best fits this presentation?
options: Milk-alkali syndrome (exogenous calcium overload)
Familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia
Granulomatous disease
CYP24A1 deficiency
items.explanation: Excessive oral calcium ingestion causing hypercalcemia, alkalosis, and renal insufficiency is the classic milk-alkali syndrome, an example of exogenous calcium overload.
items.reference: ETIOLOGY (Hypercalcemia): "as does exogenous calcium overload. The latter is most commonly observed in excessive oral ingestion (e.g., milk-alkali syndrome) or during total parenteral nutrition with excessive calcium supplementation."

items.question: A bedridden elderly man develops hypercalcemia attributed to prolonged immobilization. What should the clinician consider in this setting?
options: A previously unrecognized contributor to bone resorption, such as pHPT, Paget's disease, or myeloma
Immobilization alone cannot cause hypercalcemia in adults, so the diagnosis must be reconsidered
Immobilization hypercalcemia is always due to occult vitamin D intoxication
No further workup is needed once immobilization is identified as a possible cause
items.explanation: Immobilization increases osteoclastic bone resorption via increased osteocyte RANKL, but in older patients a previously unrecognized contributor such as pHPT, Paget's disease, or myeloma should be considered.
items.reference: ETIOLOGY (Hypercalcemia): "Hypercalcemia associated with prolonged immobilization arises from reduced mechanical loading of bone that increases osteocyte RANKL production and osteoclast-mediated bone resorption. In this setting, particularly with older patients, a previously unrecognized contributor to bone resorption (e.g., pHPT, Paget's disease, or myeloma) may be present and should be considered."

items.question: A patient has a total serum calcium of 11.6 mg/dL and a serum albumin of 2.0 g/dL (reference value 4.0 g/dL). What is the albumin-corrected serum calcium?
options: 13.2 mg/dL
11.6 mg/dL
10.0 mg/dL
12.4 mg/dL
items.explanation: Albumin is 2.0 g/dL below the 4.0 g/dL reference value, so 0.8 mg/dL is added for each 1.0 g/dL decrement: 2 x 0.8 = 1.6 mg/dL added to 11.6, giving a corrected calcium of 13.2 mg/dL.
items.reference: DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (Hypercalcemia): "When serum albumin concentrations are reduced, a corrected calcium concentration is calculated by adding 0.8 mg/dL to the total calcium level for every decrement in serum albumin of 1.0 g/dL below the albumin reference value of 4.0 g/dL."

items.question: A patient presents with severe symptomatic hypercalcemia (serum calcium 15 mg/dL) and clinical dehydration. What is the most appropriate initial step in management?
options: Intravenous isotonic saline for volume expansion
Immediate loop diuretic administration
Intravenous zoledronic acid alone, before any fluids
Emergency hemodialysis
items.explanation: Initial therapy for significant hypercalcemia begins with volume expansion (often 4-6 L IV saline over 24 h) because hypercalcemia invariably causes dehydration; loop diuretics should not be started until volume status is restored.
items.reference: TREATMENT — Hypercalcemia: "Initial therapy of significant hypercalcemia begins with volume expansion because hypercalcemia invariably leads to dehydration, as noted above; 4-6 L of intravenous saline may be required over the first 24 h."

items.question: A patient with hypercalcemia of malignancy receives intravenous zoledronic acid after volume repletion. What is the expected timeline and likelihood of normalizing serum calcium?
options: Onset within 1-2 days, with normalization in 60-90% of patients
Onset within minutes of the infusion
Effect is not expected for 2-3 weeks
Aminobisphosphonates have no significant effect on hypercalcemia of malignancy
items.explanation: The onset of action of IV aminobisphosphonates is within 1-2 days, with normalization of serum calcium in 60-90% of patients.
items.reference: TREATMENT — Hypercalcemia: "The onset of intravenous aminobisphosphonate action is within 1-2 days, with normalization of serum calcium levels occurring in 60-90% of patients."

items.question: A patient with hypercalcemia of malignancy and a GFR of 20 mL/min/1.73 m^2 needs bone-resorption-targeted therapy. Which agent is most appropriate, and why?
options: Denosumab, because it is cleared by the reticuloendothelial system rather than the kidney
Zoledronic acid at the standard dose, because it is renally cleared and effective
Pamidronate at double the standard dose to compensate for reduced clearance
Calcitonin alone as definitive long-term therapy
items.explanation: Intravenous aminobisphosphonates are contraindicated with GFR <35 mL/min/1.73 m^2, but denosumab (at a lower dose, e.g., 0.3 mg/kg) is cleared by the reticuloendothelial system and can be used in advanced kidney disease.
items.reference: TREATMENT — Hypercalcemia: "Lower doses of denosumab (e.g., 0.3 mg/kg) can be used to treat hypercalcemia patients in the setting of advanced kidney disease, since intravenous aminobisphosphonates are contraindicated with glomerular filtration rate <35 mL/min/1.73 m^2 and denosumab is cleared by the reticuloendothelial system."

items.question: A patient is diagnosed with parathyroid carcinoma causing PTH-driven hypercalcemia. Which drug, acting as a CaSR agonist, is used to manage this?
options: Cinacalcet
Denosumab
Teriparatide
Calcitriol
items.explanation: Parathyroid carcinoma causing hypercalcemia of malignancy is treated with the CaSR agonist cinacalcet, starting at 30 mg orally twice daily and titrated to normalize serum calcium.
items.reference: TREATMENT — Hypercalcemia: "When diagnosed, parathyroid carcinoma, a rare cause of hypercalcemia of malignancy driven by PTH, is treated with the CaSR agonist cinacalcet, starting with 30 mg orally twice daily and titrated to normalize serum calcium."

items.question: A patient with sarcoidosis-related hypercalcemia and an elevated 1,25(OH)2D is started on therapy. Which medication class is foundational for this etiology?
options: Glucocorticoids
Loop diuretics
Aminobisphosphonates as sole therapy
Cinacalcet
items.explanation: Glucocorticoids are foundational pharmacotherapy for vitamin D- or 1,25(OH)2D-mediated hypercalcemia because they reduce intestinal calcium absorption and decrease 1,25(OH)2D production.
items.reference: TREATMENT — Hypercalcemia: "In patients with vitamin D- or 1,25(OH)2D-mediated hypercalcemia, glucocorticoids represent foundational pharmacotherapy, as they directly reduce calcium absorption from the small intestine and decrease 1,25(OH)2D production."

items.question: A patient with hypercalcemia of malignancy has a GFR of 28 mL/min/1.73 m^2. Which statement about aminobisphosphonate therapy is correct?
options: Intravenous aminobisphosphonates are contraindicated with GFR <35 mL/min/1.73 m^2
Aminobisphosphonates require no dose adjustment regardless of renal function
Aminobisphosphonates are preferred over denosumab in advanced kidney disease
Aminobisphosphonates should be given at higher doses to compensate for reduced clearance
items.explanation: Intravenous aminobisphosphonates are contraindicated with a GFR below 35 mL/min/1.73 m^2; denosumab is preferred (at lower dose) in this setting because it is cleared by the reticuloendothelial system.
items.reference: TREATMENT — Hypercalcemia: "intravenous aminobisphosphonates are contraindicated with glomerular filtration rate <35 mL/min/1.73 m^2 and denosumab is cleared by the reticuloendothelial system."

items.question: Which ECG finding is most characteristic of significant hypercalcemia?
options: Short QTc interval
Prolonged QTc interval
Peaked T waves
Delta wave
items.explanation: Hypercalcemia can cause bradycardia, atrioventricular block, QRS widening, and a short QTc interval — the opposite of the QTc prolongation seen with hypocalcemia.
items.reference: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (Hypercalcemia): "Finally, hypercalcemia can result in significant electrocardiographic changes, including bradycardia, atrioventricular block, QRS widening, and short QTc interval."

items.question: A patient has hypercalcemia with a suppressed PTH, and no malignancy is obvious on initial evaluation. Which test may be needed to establish the diagnosis of hypercalcemia of malignancy?
options: Serum PTHrP level
Serum calcitonin level
24-hour urinary fractional excretion of calcium for FHH
Genetic testing for CASR mutations
items.explanation: Although a tumor causing hypercalcemia is generally overt, a PTHrP level may be needed to establish the diagnosis of hypercalcemia of malignancy.
items.reference: DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (Hypercalcemia): "Although a tumor that causes hypercalcemia is generally overt, a PTHrP level may be needed to establish the diagnosis of hypercalcemia of malignancy."

items.question: A patient with hypercalcemia and suppressed PTH is being evaluated for multiple myeloma. Serum and urine protein electrophoresis with immunofixation are negative. What additional test should be performed?
options: Serum free light chains, since ~3% of myeloma may be undetected by electrophoresis with immunofixation
Repeat PTH testing only
PTHrP level only, since myeloma cannot otherwise be excluded
No further testing is needed if electrophoresis is negative
items.explanation: Multiple myeloma should be evaluated with serum free light chains in addition to electrophoresis with immunofixation, since ~3% of myeloma may otherwise be undetected.
items.reference: DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (Hypercalcemia): "Multiple myeloma must be evaluated in this setting as well, including measurement of serum free light chains since ~3% of myeloma may be undetected by serum or urine protein electrophoresis with immunofixation."

items.question: Two days after total thyroidectomy, a patient develops perioral numbness, carpal spasm, and a positive Trousseau's sign with a serum calcium of 6.5 mg/dL. What is the most appropriate immediate treatment?
options: IV calcium gluconate with telemetry
Oral calcium carbonate alone
Oral calcitriol alone, with calcium to follow in one week
Observation, since symptoms typically resolve spontaneously within 24 hours
items.explanation: Acute symptomatic hypocalcemia is initially managed with IV calcium gluconate given over 5-10 minutes with telemetry, with repeat dosing or continuous infusion as needed.
items.reference: HYPOCALCEMIA — TREATMENT: "Acute, symptomatic hypocalcemia is initially managed with calcium gluconate, a 10-mL ampule of 10% wt/vol (90 mg or 2.2 mmol) diluted in 50 mL of 5% dextrose or 0.9% sodium chloride and given intravenously over 5-10 min with telemetry."

items.question: A patient with hypocalcemia due to hypomagnesemia from chronic proton pump inhibitor use shows only transient improvement with IV calcium. What is essential to add to the treatment plan?
options: Magnesium supplementation
A higher rate of calcium infusion alone
Cinacalcet
Denosumab
items.explanation: Hypomagnesemia impairs PTH secretion and causes hypocalcemia; accompanying hypomagnesemia must be corrected with magnesium supplementation for hypocalcemia to resolve.
items.reference: HYPOCALCEMIA — TREATMENT: "Accompanying hypomagnesemia, if present, should be treated with appropriate magnesium supplementation, with initiation of chronic-phase treatment once tetany has resolved, the patient is stable and able to safely take oral medication, and the QTc has normalized."

items.question: On examination, tapping a patient's facial nerve anterior to the ear elicits twitching of the perioral muscles (Chvostek's sign). How should this finding be interpreted?
options: It reflects neuromuscular irritability of hypocalcemia but is also present in ~10% of normal individuals
It is pathognomonic for hypocalcemia and never occurs in normal individuals
It indicates hypercalcemia rather than hypocalcemia
It is a definitive sign of impending tetany requiring no further testing
items.explanation: Chvostek's sign reflects increased neuromuscular irritability seen in hypocalcemia, but it is also present in approximately 10% of normal individuals, so it is not definitive on its own.
items.reference: HYPOCALCEMIA — CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS: "a Chvostek's sign (twitching of the ipsilateral perioral levator and zygomaticus muscles in response to gentle tapping of the facial nerve, just anterior to the ear or between the corner of the mouth and zygomatic arch) may be elicited, although it is also present in ~10% of normal individuals."

items.question: A patient with chronic hypoparathyroidism is managed with oral calcium and calcitriol. What is the appropriate serum calcium target, and why?
options: Albumin-corrected low-normal serum calcium, because normalizing calcium above low-normal without PTH replacement increases hypercalciuria risk
Mid-normal serum calcium, regardless of urine calcium
High-normal serum calcium, to prevent any recurrence of tetany
There is no specific serum calcium target as long as symptoms are absent
items.explanation: Because distal tubular calcium reabsorption is PTH-dependent, oral calcium and calcitriol should be titrated to an albumin-corrected low-normal serum calcium; raising calcium further without PTH replacement increases hypercalciuria, nephrolithiasis, and nephrocalcinosis risk.
items.reference: HYPOCALCEMIA — TREATMENT: "with careful titration of oral calcitriol [1,25(OH)2D, 0.25-1 ug/d] to achieve albumin-corrected low-normal serum calcium levels. Because calcium resorption in the distal convoluted tubule (about 10% of the filtered load) is dependent upon PTH, normalization of calcium to above the low-normal range in the absence of PTH replacement increases hypercalciuria with risk of nephrolithiasis and nephrocalcinosis."

items.question: A patient with hypoparathyroidism refractory to high-dose oral calcium and calcitriol, with recurrent hypercalciuria, is considered for PTH replacement. Which agent has been used off-label for this indication?
options: Teriparatide (PTH 1-34), 20 ug subcutaneously twice daily
PTH (1-84) (Natpara), which remains first-line and widely available
Denosumab
Cinacalcet
items.explanation: Teriparatide (PTH 1-34), approved for osteoporosis, has been used off-label at 20 ug SC twice daily for refractory hypoparathyroidism; Natpara (PTH 1-84) has been discontinued by its manufacturer.
items.reference: HYPOCALCEMIA — TREATMENT: "PTH (1-34), also known as teriparatide, is approved for osteoporosis, but has been successfully used off-label (20 ug subcutaneously twice daily) for treating refractory hypoparathyroidism."
