items.question: A patient with hypocalcemia following Roux-en-Y bariatric surgery requires calcium supplementation. Which formulation is preferred, and why?
options: Calcium citrate, because it is better absorbed and increases urinary citrate to reduce nephrolithiasis risk from increased oxalate absorption
Calcium carbonate, because it requires no gastric acid for absorption
Calcium phosphate, to also correct any coexisting hypophosphatemia
Oral calcium supplementation is not effective after bariatric surgery
items.explanation: In intestinal disease such as after Roux-en-Y bypass, calcium citrate is preferred because it is better absorbed and increases urinary citrate, mitigating the increased nephrolithiasis risk from oxalate absorption.
items.reference: HYPOCALCEMIA — TREATMENT: "In the setting of intestinal disease, calcium supplementation as calcium citrate is preferred because it is better absorbed, and this formulation increases urinary citrate to mitigate the risk of nephrolithiasis in this setting due to increased dietary oxalate absorption."

items.question: A patient with severe enteric malabsorption after Roux-en-Y bypass has hypocalcemia from vitamin D deficiency. Compared with nutritional vitamin D deficiency, what dosing strategy is typically required?
options: Much higher doses, such as ergocalciferol carefully titrated up to 100,000 IU/d
The same moderate dose as nutritional deficiency (50,000 IU 2-3 times weekly)
A single one-time dose of 50,000 IU is always sufficient
Vitamin D supplementation is contraindicated after bariatric surgery
items.explanation: Vitamin D deficiency due to severe enteric malabsorption, as after Roux-en-Y bariatric surgery, may require much higher doses such as ergocalciferol carefully titrated up to 100,000 IU/d, compared with the 50,000 IU 2-3 times weekly typically used for nutritional deficiency.
items.reference: HYPOCALCEMIA — TREATMENT: "nutritional vitamin D deficiency generally responds to moderate doses of oral vitamin D (e.g., ergocalciferol at 50,000 IU, 2-3 times per week for several months), whereas vitamin D deficiency due to severe enteric malabsorption may require much higher doses (e.g., ergocalciferol carefully titrated up to 100,000 IU/d), as is often the case following Roux-en-Y bariatric surgery."

items.statement: Primary hyperparathyroidism and malignancy are the two most common causes of hypercalcemia.
items.explanation: Per Harrison's, primary hyperparathyroidism and malignancy are the two most common causes of hypercalcemia, although other diseases must be considered.
items.reference: ETIOLOGY (Hypercalcemia): "Primary hyperparathyroidism and malignancy are the two most common causes of hypercalcemia, although other diseases must be considered (Table 57-2)."

items.statement: A calcium/creatinine clearance ratio greater than 0.02 is suggestive of familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia.
items.explanation: A calcium/creatinine clearance ratio below 0.01 — not above 0.02 — is suggestive of FHH; a fractional excretion of calcium above 0.02 instead supports pHPT.
items.reference: DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (Hypercalcemia): "a calcium/creatinine clearance ratio (calculated as urine calcium/serum calcium divided by urine creatinine/serum creatinine) of <0.01 is suggestive of FHH."

items.statement: Hypercalcemia of malignancy is typically associated with an elevated PTH level.
items.explanation: In hypercalcemia of malignancy, PTH levels are suppressed because high serum calcium activates the CaSR to inhibit PTH production; the hypercalcemia is mediated by factors such as PTHrP rather than by PTH itself.
items.reference: ETIOLOGY (Hypercalcemia): "In hypercalcemic disorders with normal parathyroid function, PTH levels are suppressed by high serum calcium levels that activate the CaSR to inhibit parathyroid gland PTH production... This is frequently encountered in patients with advanced malignancy."

items.statement: The hypercalcemia of granulomatous disease results from extrarenal conversion of 25(OH)D to 1,25(OH)2D by macrophage CYP27B1 activity.
items.explanation: Macrophages within granulomas express CYP27B1, which converts 25(OH)D to the more potent 1,25(OH)2D, enhancing intestinal calcium absorption and producing hypercalcemia with suppressed PTH.
items.reference: ETIOLOGY (Hypercalcemia): "The hypercalcemia associated with lymphoma or with granulomatous disease (e.g., sarcoidosis, tuberculosis, foreign body reaction) is caused by extrarenal conversion of 25(OH)D to the more potent pro-calcific hormone 1,25(OH)2D by macrophage CYP27B1 activity."

items.statement: Loop diuretics should be initiated before volume status has been restored to normal in the treatment of significant hypercalcemia.
items.explanation: Although loop diuretics can transiently augment calciuresis, they should not be initiated until volume status has been restored to normal — volume expansion with IV saline comes first.
items.reference: TREATMENT — Hypercalcemia: "Although loop diuretics can be transiently used to further augment sodium and thus calcium excretion by the distal tubule, loop diuretics should not be initiated until the volume status has been restored to normal."

items.statement: Intravenous aminobisphosphonates are contraindicated with a glomerular filtration rate below 35 mL/min/1.73 m^2.
items.explanation: Aminobisphosphonates are contraindicated with GFR <35 mL/min/1.73 m^2; denosumab, cleared by the reticuloendothelial system, is an alternative in this setting.
items.reference: TREATMENT — Hypercalcemia: "intravenous aminobisphosphonates are contraindicated with glomerular filtration rate <35 mL/min/1.73 m^2 and denosumab is cleared by the reticuloendothelial system."

items.statement: Cinacalcet, a calcium-sensing receptor agonist, is used to treat parathyroid carcinoma and pHPT patients who are not candidates for parathyroid surgery.
items.explanation: Cinacalcet is used for parathyroid carcinoma-related hypercalcemia and is also used in pHPT patients who are not candidates for parathyroid gland surgery.
items.reference: TREATMENT — Hypercalcemia: "is treated with the CaSR agonist cinacalcet, starting with 30 mg orally twice daily and titrated to normalize serum calcium. Of note, cinacalcet is also used to treat patients with pHPT who are not candidates for parathyroid gland surgery."

items.statement: In adults, hypoparathyroidism most commonly results from inadvertent damage to the parathyroid glands during thyroid or parathyroid surgery.
items.explanation: Inadvertent damage to all four parathyroid glands during neck surgery is the most common adult cause of hypoparathyroidism.
items.reference: HYPOCALCEMIA — ETIOLOGY: "In adults, hypoparathyroidism most commonly results from inadvertent damage to all four parathyroid glands during thyroid or parathyroid gland surgery."

items.statement: Severe hypermagnesemia, such as from obstetric magnesium infusions, can suppress PTH release and cause hypocalcemia.
items.explanation: Because extracellular magnesium binds the CaSR with about one-third the affinity of calcium, severe hypermagnesemia (e.g., obstetric magnesium infusions) can suppress PTH release and rarely causes symptomatic hypoparathyroidism.
items.reference: HYPOCALCEMIA — ETIOLOGY: "severe hypermagnesemia arising from obstetric magnesium infusions for preeclampsia or preterm labor can also suppress PTH release; this rarely causes symptomatic hypoparathyroidism that must be managed until the magnesium infusion is discontinued."

items.subtopic: Vitamin D Deficiency — Severity of Hypocalcemia
items.statement: The degree of hypocalcemia in vitamin D deficiency is usually as severe as that seen in hypoparathyroidism, because the parathyroid glands cannot compensate.
items.explanation: Hypocalcemia from vitamin D deficiency or CKD is usually less severe than in hypoparathyroidism, because intact parathyroids can mount a compensatory increase in PTH that buffers the fall in calcium.
items.reference: HYPOCALCEMIA — ETIOLOGY: "the degree of hypocalcemia in these disorders is most often not as severe as that seen with hypoparathyroidism; this is because the parathyroids are capable of mounting a compensatory increase in PTH secretion that buffers the extent of hypocalcemia to the detriment of bone."

items.statement: Chvostek's sign is present in approximately 10% of normal individuals.
items.explanation: Chvostek's sign reflects neuromuscular irritability and is found in hypocalcemia, but it is also present in roughly 10% of individuals with normal calcium levels.
items.reference: HYPOCALCEMIA — CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS: "a Chvostek's sign... may be elicited, although it is also present in ~10% of normal individuals."

items.statement: Normalizing serum calcium above the low-normal range without PTH replacement in hypoparathyroidism increases the risk of hypercalciuria and nephrocalcinosis.
items.explanation: Because distal tubular calcium reabsorption is PTH-dependent, raising serum calcium above the low-normal range without PTH replacement increases hypercalciuria, with risk of nephrolithiasis and nephrocalcinosis.
items.reference: HYPOCALCEMIA — TREATMENT: "Because calcium resorption in the distal convoluted tubule (about 10% of the filtered load) is dependent upon PTH, normalization of calcium to above the low-normal range in the absence of PTH replacement increases hypercalciuria with risk of nephrolithiasis and nephrocalcinosis."

items.assertion: In a patient with hypercalcemia and a mildly elevated PTH plus a family history of mild asymptomatic hypercalcemia, FHH should be excluded before referring for parathyroid surgery.
items.reason: Parathyroid surgery is ineffective in familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion: because parathyroid surgery does not correct FHH, it must be excluded first, especially with a suggestive family history.
items.reference: DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (Hypercalcemia): "Because individuals with FHH may also present with mildly elevated PTH levels and hypercalcemia, FHH should be considered and excluded because parathyroid surgery is ineffective in this condition."

items.assertion: Hypercalcemia due to sarcoidosis is typically accompanied by a suppressed PTH and an elevated or inappropriately normal serum 1,25(OH)2D.
items.reason: Macrophages within granulomas express CYP27B1, which converts 25(OH)D to the more potent 1,25(OH)2D.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason explains the assertion: macrophage CYP27B1 activity drives extrarenal 1,25(OH)2D production, causing hypercalcemia with suppressed PTH and elevated/inappropriately normal 1,25(OH)2D.
items.reference: ETIOLOGY (Hypercalcemia): "The hypercalcemia associated with lymphoma or with granulomatous disease (e.g., sarcoidosis, tuberculosis, foreign body reaction) is caused by extrarenal conversion of 25(OH)D to the more potent pro-calcific hormone 1,25(OH)2D by macrophage CYP27B1 activity... this chronic 1,25(OH)D production enhances intestinal calcium absorption, resulting in hypercalcemia and suppressed PTH."

items.assertion: Loop diuretics are recommended as first-line therapy for significant hypercalcemia, before volume repletion.
items.reason: Loop diuretics augment sodium and calcium excretion by the distal tubule.
items.explanation: The assertion is false: initial therapy begins with volume expansion, and loop diuretics should not be initiated until volume status has been restored to normal. The reason is true — loop diuretics do augment distal sodium and calcium excretion — but it does not justify using them first.
items.reference: TREATMENT — Hypercalcemia: "Although loop diuretics can be transiently used to further augment sodium and thus calcium excretion by the distal tubule, loop diuretics should not be initiated until the volume status has been restored to normal."

items.assertion: Denosumab is preferred over intravenous aminobisphosphonates for treating hypercalcemia in patients with advanced kidney disease.
items.reason: Denosumab is cleared by the reticuloendothelial system rather than the kidney, whereas aminobisphosphonates are contraindicated with a GFR below 35 mL/min/1.73 m^2.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason explains the assertion: because denosumab clearance does not depend on the kidney while aminobisphosphonates are contraindicated in advanced kidney disease, denosumab (at a lower dose) is preferred in this setting.
items.reference: TREATMENT — Hypercalcemia: "Lower doses of denosumab (e.g., 0.3 mg/kg) can be used to treat hypercalcemia patients in the setting of advanced kidney disease, since intravenous aminobisphosphonates are contraindicated with glomerular filtration rate <35 mL/min/1.73 m^2 and denosumab is cleared by the reticuloendothelial system."

items.assertion: Hypercalcemia from vitamin D intoxication typically resolves within a day or two of stopping the offending agent.
items.reason: 1,25(OH)D is normally metabolized within a day or two.
items.explanation: The assertion is false: the longer half-lives of vitamin D and 25(OH)D can prolong hypercalcemia from vitamin D intoxication for several weeks, despite 1,25(OH)D itself being metabolized within a day or two. The reason, taken alone, is true.
items.reference: ETIOLOGY (Hypercalcemia): "While 1,25(OH)D is normally metabolized within a day or two, the longer half-lives of vitamin D and 25(OH)D can prolong hypercalcemia from vitamin D intoxication for several weeks."

items.assertion: A calcium/creatinine clearance ratio below 0.01 supports a diagnosis of FHH rather than pHPT.
items.reason: FHH mutations impair extracellular calcium sensing in both the parathyroid gland and the kidney.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason explains the assertion: impaired renal CaSR function in FHH causes inappropriately low urinary calcium excretion relative to serum calcium, producing a calcium/creatinine clearance ratio below 0.01.
items.reference: DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (Hypercalcemia): "Due to impaired CaSR function in parathyroids and kidney, a calcium/creatinine clearance ratio (calculated as urine calcium/serum calcium divided by urine creatinine/serum creatinine) of <0.01 is suggestive of FHH... All FHH mutations impair extracellular calcium sensing in both the parathyroid gland and the kidneys, leading to concomitant inappropriate PTH secretion and decreased urinary calcium excretion."

items.assertion: Magnesium deficiency is a common cause of hypercalcemia.
items.reason: Magnesium deficiency impairs PTH secretion via intracellular actions.
items.explanation: The assertion is false: magnesium deficiency causes hypocalcemia, not hypercalcemia, because impaired PTH secretion reduces the main minute-to-minute defense against falling calcium. The reason is true on its own.
items.reference: HYPOCALCEMIA — ETIOLOGY: "More commonly, impaired PTH secretion may be secondary to magnesium deficiency (via intracellular actions)."

items.assertion: Late-onset adult idiopathic hypoparathyroidism, unrelated to neck surgery or autoimmune polyglandular syndrome type 1, frequently exhibits autoimmunity that targets the CaSR and other parathyroid antigens.
items.reason: Autoimmune endocrinopathies are responsible for about a quarter of cases of hypoparathyroidism.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion: the reason describes the overall contribution of autoimmune endocrinopathies to hypoparathyroidism, whereas the assertion describes a distinct finding of CaSR-targeted autoimmunity specifically in idiopathic, non-APS-1, non-surgical late-onset cases.
items.reference: HYPOCALCEMIA — ETIOLOGY: "Hypoparathyroidism is a cardinal feature of autoimmune endocrinopathies (Chap. 400), responsible for about a quarter of hypoparathyroidism. Even late-onset adult idiopathic hypoparathyroidism—unrelated to neck surgery or autoimmune poly-glandular syndrome type 1 (APS-1)—frequently exhibits autoimmunity that targets CaSR and other parathyroid antigens."

items.assertion: Chvostek's sign is elicited by tapping the facial nerve anterior to the ear, producing twitching of the perioral and zygomatic muscles.
items.reason: Chvostek's sign is never present in individuals with normal serum calcium.
items.explanation: The assertion is true and correctly describes how Chvostek's sign is elicited. The reason is false: Chvostek's sign is present in approximately 10% of normal individuals, so it is not specific to hypocalcemia.
items.reference: HYPOCALCEMIA — CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS: "a Chvostek's sign (twitching of the ipsilateral perioral levator and zygomaticus muscles in response to gentle tapping of the facial nerve, just anterior to the ear or between the corner of the mouth and zygomatic arch) may be elicited, although it is also present in ~10% of normal individuals."

items.assertion: In chronic hypoparathyroidism, oral calcium and calcitriol should be titrated to achieve a mid-normal serum calcium level.
items.reason: Distal convoluted tubule calcium reabsorption is PTH-dependent, so raising serum calcium above the low-normal range without PTH replacement increases the risk of hypercalciuria.
items.explanation: The assertion is false: the target is an albumin-corrected low-normal, not mid-normal, serum calcium. The reason is true and is in fact the rationale for why a higher (mid-normal) target would be inappropriate without PTH replacement.
items.reference: HYPOCALCEMIA — TREATMENT: "with careful titration of oral calcitriol [1,25(OH)2D, 0.25-1 ug/d] to achieve albumin-corrected low-normal serum calcium levels. Because calcium resorption in the distal convoluted tubule (about 10% of the filtered load) is dependent upon PTH, normalization of calcium to above the low-normal range in the absence of PTH replacement increases hypercalciuria with risk of nephrolithiasis and nephrocalcinosis."

items.assertion: PTH (1-84) (Natpara) remains a first-line, widely available FDA-approved replacement therapy for hypoparathyroidism.
items.reason: Teriparatide (PTH 1-34) is FDA-approved specifically for the treatment of hypoparathyroidism.
items.explanation: Both statements are false: Natpara has been discontinued by its manufacturer, and teriparatide is FDA-approved for osteoporosis, not hypoparathyroidism, though it has been used off-label for refractory hypoparathyroidism.
items.reference: HYPOCALCEMIA — TREATMENT: "PTH (1-84) (Natpara), approved by the Food and Drug Administration for treatment of hypoparathyroidism, has now been discontinued by the manufacturer. PTH (1-34), also known as teriparatide, is approved for osteoporosis, but has been successfully used off-label (20 ug subcutaneously twice daily) for treating refractory hypoparathyroidism."

items.assertion: In vitamin D- or 1,25(OH)2D-mediated hypercalcemia, serum phosphate is frequently elevated or high-normal.
items.reason: 1,25(OH)2D increases intestinal phosphate absorption and reduces renal phosphate excretion.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason explains the assertion: increased intestinal absorption and reduced renal excretion of phosphate driven by 1,25(OH)2D account for the elevated or high-normal serum phosphate seen in vitamin D-mediated hypercalcemia.
items.reference: DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (Hypercalcemia): "With vitamin D- or 1,25(OH)2D-mediated hypercalcemia, serum phosphate is frequently elevated or high normal due to increased gastrointestinal absorption and reduced renal excretion."
